title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in our local awa (stream) - Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, which flows through our Ngāti Kahungunu rohe. Students will explore the connection between the health of our waterway and the hauora (wellbeing) of our community, drawing on traditional knowledge of how our tūpuna (ancestors) cared for this precious resource as a source of food, medicine, and spiritual sustenance.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain three traditional Māori practices used to maintain the mauri (life force) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Conduct water quality testing using both scientific instruments and mātauranga Māori observation methods
Design and implement a practical kaitiakitanga action plan to address an identified issue affecting the stream's health
Present findings to local kaumātua demonstrating understanding of the connection between environmental and community wellbeing

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa - Story of Our Stream
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua to hear pūrākau (traditional stories) about the stream's significance, learn traditional names for different sections, and understand historical uses for food gathering and rongoā (medicine)

activities.title: Mauri Meter - Assessing Stream Health
activities.description: Practical session where students use water testing kits (pH, nitrate levels) alongside mātauranga Māori assessment methods (observing insect life, water clarity, plant health) to evaluate the current state of the awa

activities.title: Rāhui in Action - Understanding Protection Practices
activities.description: Workshop with local kaitiaki to understand the concept of rāhui (temporary prohibition) and how it protects resources, followed by designing modern-day protection measures for vulnerable areas of the stream

activities.title: Hauora Connections - Stream to Community Wellness
activities.description: Students map how the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku directly impacts community wellbeing - from food sources and recreational swimming to spiritual connection and cultural identity

activities.title: Kaitiaki Challenge - Action Project Implementation
activities.description: Students work in groups to implement a practical restoration project (native planting, creating educational signage, organizing community clean-up) and monitor its impact over two weeks

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu Marae
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water testing kits and monitoring guides
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Māori concepts of water rights and water protection'
Traditional fishing nets and implements from college's cultural collection

tasks: Create a detailed kaitiakitanga portfolio documenting stream assessment findings
Develop and present a restoration proposal to local marae representatives
Produce a reflective journal connecting personal actions to broader environmental and community wellbeing

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A3 - Safety management; B3 - Interpersonal skills; C3 - Community resources; D3 - Societal attitudes and values
Social Sciences: Understand how people manage resources and environments
Science: Living World - Ecology achievement objectives
Tikanga ā-Iwi: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
